finding: Perceived reduction in victim humanity (agency, experience, emotional capacity) predicts support for instrumental harm (harming to achieve goals unrelated to justice) but not moral violence (harming those perceived as deserving). The mechanism is bidirectional: dehumanization enables instrumental violence via apathy; moral violence requires recognition of humanity to justify blame.

Exposure to factual information about rising economic inequality increases perception that structural barriers (wealth, education) matter for economic success and decreases emphasis on individual effort, thereby increasing support for both government redistribution and business responsibility to reduce pay disparity—effects mediated by shifts in opportunity beliefs.

Temporary-sharing media reduce privacy concerns and increase disinhibition in disclosed content, but observers' judgments of sharers are insensitive to platform permanence and instead driven by content disinhibition. Sharers mistakenly believe platform temporariness will improve impressions, creating a mismatch between disclosure incentives and social consequences.

Individuals who compare attributes across choice options (comparative search) exhibit greater patience in intertemporal choices and larger susceptibility to framing effects, whereas individuals who integrate information within options show lower patience and reduced framing sensitivity. Manipulating ease of search strategies causally shifts both patience and framing effects.

Brief training in cognitive reappraisal strategies (emphasizing negative aspects of unhealthy foods or positive aspects of healthy foods) increases subsequent healthy food choices by 5-11%, reduces caloric consumption by 93-121 calories, and generalizes to untrained foods and real food, with effects independent of participant BMI.

Bipartisan social networks without partisan identity cues enable social learning that eliminates baseline partisan bias in interpretation of climate data for both conservatives and liberals; however, increasing salience of partisan identity through party logos or peer political labels significantly reduces social learning and maintains polarization.
